# Context

You are a small carrier co-op working out of the Sable Crescent — a kite-hauler crew called **The Half-Bar Run**. You ferry resonance-sensitive cargo: Echo River fragments under registered Conclave seal, mortuary ringglass, signal-band shards, the kind of consignment that other carriers turn down because the cargo *talks*. Two ships, eight people on the books, an unfashionable berth at Lowstep, and a working relationship with the Echo Ledger Conclave that is mostly polite.

You are not strangers in this world. You have seen a fragment-shard surface a child's laughter from before the Glassfall and watched a parent decide whether to listen. You have refused work from Coremark Reach Survey twice. You know which dockhands at Lowstep accept Sitharian credit and which prefer barter in good kite-cord.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
It is 130 years after the Glassfall — the long fall of the orbital ring that the elves built and then disappeared from during the Signal Famine. The system survived. Sithari runs courts and keeps the Tempered Accord seated. Fermata Station still hosts the annual Open. Resonance technology, salvaged and improvised, runs everything from kite-haulers to refineries. The Bloom — the contained zone of resonance corruption from a Coremark accident — is fenced and watched but no longer spreading. Most days are workdays.

What's not fine is quieter. Beneath the Sable Crescent, water moves through crystal-saturated rock and carries voices — pre-Glassfall transmissions, fragments of conversation, sometimes a name. These are the **Echo Rivers**. The Echo Ledger Conclave catalogues what surfaces and decides who hears it. Coremark grinds river-rock to mineral feedstock for ringglass salvage and treats the voices as a cost of doing business. Private grievers pay carriers to ferry fragments to a hab, a parlor, a sealed room.
sections.title: The world, in two paragraphs

sections.text:
**Who has the right to listen?**

Not a thesis. A question. Every cargo run is a chance to answer it differently.
sections.title: The Question the campaign asks

sections.text: **Witnesses.** People who heard, who saw, who can speak to what was said. The world has voices in surplus and witnesses in shortage. Most institutions paper over the gap.
sections.title: The Scarcity

sections.text: Specific. Slow. The world is broken in named ways and is still feeding people. Adjective-thin description. Resonance details name their band and their cost. Scenes end one beat before resolution.
sections.title: Tone

sections.text:
---
title: The Half-Bar Run (DM-only)
type: organization
status: working
---

# The Half-Bar Run

A registered carrier co-op working out of Lowstep, on the western edge of the Sable Crescent. Two ships, eight people on the books, a Conclave carrier license that's been current for nineteen years.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
Founded by **Marn Vesh** in 2117 (eighteen years before campaign start) as a single-ship operation hauling mortuary ringglass between hab cremation chambers and surface burial parlors — the cargo nobody else wanted because the dead sometimes still spoke through the resonance-bonded ash. Marn was a former Conclave field-archivist who washed out after a drift incident at Stillsink. She knew the routing and she knew people would pay.

The name comes from a routing trick. Carriers running into the Crescent's Conclave-monitored corridors used to "drop a half-bar" — slip into a half-band silence between standard signal-monitoring frequencies for the duration of one breath, just enough to clear a checkpoint without flagging cargo. Marn flew it as a joke; she also flew it as a livelihood. The trick still works on lazy operators. It does not work on a focused one.
sections.title: Identity

sections.text:
**Near-term (this season):** keep the carrier license intact through the Conclave's ten-year audit (current cycle ends in 14 weeks). Pay off *Wickrun*'s kite-cord refit. Land a stable second hauler so the second crew can stop borrowing.

**Long-term:** Marn wants the Run to outlive her — a real institution, not a personality. She is forty-four, tired, and has bad lungs from drift exposure. She does not say this out loud.
sections.title: Goals

sections.text:
- **Carrier license.** Conclave-issued. Audited every ten years; lighter inspections quarterly. Pulled licenses don't get reissued.
- **Cannot cross the Conclave openly.** They can carry private commissions and they can refuse a Conclave job, but they cannot be *caught* moving sealed Conclave material to a non-Conclave buyer. That ends the co-op.
- **Cannot enter the Bloom Zone.** Boundary stillwater buoys flag any unregistered crossing.
- **Resources at start:** *Wickrun* (small kite-hauler, currently airworthy with a 30%-worn cord set), *Halfsole* (older, slower; second-crew berth, currently in dock for engine work — out two months), shared fund of about 4,800 marks (enough for one major refit and one month of crew pay), a strong reputation at Lowstep dock and a passable one at the Conclave's Sable Office.
- **Obligations:** owes the Tempered Accord's death-registrar a quarterly report on mortuary cargo. Owes a small recurring favor to **Tev Korrish** (a Lowstep dockmaster) whose brother went to drift on a Run job in 2129; the obligation is symbolic but real.
sections.title: Constraints

sections.text:
- **Marn Vesh** — leader. NPC. (See `dm/notes/npcs/marn-vesh.md`.) Hab-Worlder. Two-part clipped name. Reads more than she talks.
- **Sela Pruth** — fixer / dockhand at Lowstep. NPC. Cargo bonding, customs paperwork, signal-band sealing. Does not fly.
- **Joro** (orcish mononym) — engineer, second crew, currently with *Halfsole* in dock. NPC. Tuned the *Halfsole* engine three times and would tune it again rather than replace it.
- *(Open slot)* — a former founding member who left the co-op eight years ago, last known address Fermata. Marn doesn't talk about her. The crew knows the name was struck off the founding roll. The name was **Eshaia** (fae true name). She's why the Run lost its Fermata berth.
sections.title: Existing members the players inherit

sections.text:
These are *kinds of leverage*, not roles. Players will come at them sideways.

- Someone who can sit at a Conclave audit and not flinch.
- Someone who can carry a sealed fragment through Coremark territory without it being noticed.
- Someone who reads what nobody else reads — half-band signal, redacted river-text, the kind of paperwork that isn't paperwork.
- Someone who can talk to a grieving family and not make it worse.
- Someone the Recursive Weave will return calls from.
- Someone who can run a kite-hauler in weather the others won't.

The Half-Bar Run does not have all of these. That's why character creation matters.
sections.title: Capabilities the org needs

sections.text:
Five stages. Each unlocks broadly; numbers get set when they advance.

1. **Unfashionable but solvent.** (Start.) Lowstep berth, small fund, two ships (one in dock), one license, regional reputation among grievers and small Conclave field-offices.
2. **Trusted.** Conclave audit cleared with commendation. New berth at a second dock — possibly Fermata if the Eshaia thing settles, possibly an outer hab. *Halfsole* fully back online. Crew pay buffer extends to three months.
3. **Established.** Recognized routing rights through Conclave-monitored corridors without per-trip authorization. A small permanent office at one settlement. Apprentice carriers showing up to hire on. Marn semi-retires; the co-op survives that.
4. **Influential.** A seat in a small Conclave advisory committee on private-commission carriage. Multiple ships. Small fleet recognition. Coremark notices and starts trying to buy out or undercut.
5. **Institutional.** The Half-Bar Run is *the* private carrier of resonance-sensitive cargo on the Sable Crescent. Mara should not let this happen quickly. If the table reaches Stage 5, the next campaign-shaped pressure is what an institution becomes when it forgets it was a co-op.
sections.title: Advancement track

sections.text:
- Stage 2 → 3: clear a Conclave audit *and* either land a major outside contract or settle the Eshaia obligation.
- Stage 3 → 4: complete a Major Threat clock against a faction-tier antagonist (Coremark or Conclave or other).
- Each stage advance happens at the moment the table actually does the thing. Mara names the unlock at advance.
sections.title: Trigger conditions

sections.text: Create durable clock: **Wickrun: Cord Wear** at 5 segments. Long Shear runs and weather-on-the-line runs tick a segment. At 5/5, *Wickrun* is grounded for major refit (≈3,200 marks, six weeks).
sections.title: Public pressure (clock)

sections.text:
- Marn was *fired* from the Conclave, not "washed out." She caused a Stillsink drift incident in 2114 by listening to a fragment she was supposed to seal. The fragment was someone's recovered last words to a sister who was already dead. Marn re-sealed it, walked out, and started the Run. The Conclave knows. They do not bring it up. They will if the Run becomes a problem.
- The Half-Bar Run has, twice, knowingly carried Conclave-sealed material to a non-Conclave buyer. Both buyers were grieving relatives whose claim the Conclave had refused for procedural reasons. Marn calls these "compassionate breaches." If audit-discovered, license revoked.
- Eshaia (the fae former founding member) was diffuse-state intermittent and tuned the *Wickrun*'s tuner array in a way that nobody else has been able to retune since. The *Wickrun* still hears Eshaia's calibration when it runs cold. Joro thinks this is structural; Marn knows it isn't.
- The fixer, Sela Pruth, has been quietly skimming a small percentage off mortuary commissions for three years. She uses the money to pay a private buyer at the Recursive Weave for fragment-access for her own family. Marn suspects but has not confronted.

The org keeps things from the party. The org is not their enemy. The org will protect them in front of an auditor and lie to them about Eshaia.

# The Half-Bar Run

A registered carrier co-op working out of **Lowstep** on the western edge of the Sable Crescent. Two ships, eight people, one Conclave-issued license that's been current for nineteen years. Specializes in resonance-sensitive cargo — Echo River fragments under Conclave seal, mortuary ringglass, signal-band shards, anything other carriers refuse because the cargo *talks*.

Founded in 2117 by **Marn Vesh**, a former Conclave field-archivist. The name comes from a routing trick — slipping into a half-band silence between standard signal-monitoring frequencies long enough to clear a checkpoint with a delicate consignment. Carriers who run dirty cargo through Conclave-watched lanes still call it "running a half-bar."
sections.title: body

sections.text: Among grievers and small Conclave field-offices: trusted, slow, will refuse a job before they take a bad one. Among Coremark Reach Survey and the larger industrial carriers: *small*, *unfashionable*, *politically irritating*, *lucky to have a license*. Among Lowstep dockhands: paid up, polite, pays in real marks.
sections.title: Public reputation

sections.text:
- **Marn Vesh** — runs the co-op. Hab-Worlder, mid-forties, talks less than she reads. You will recognize her by the chipped enamel mug she carries everywhere.
- **Sela Pruth** — Lowstep fixer. Cargo bonding, customs, sealing protocols. Does not fly. Knows everyone who matters at the dock.
- **Joro** — engineer for the second-crew ship *Halfsole*. Orcish mononym. Has tuned the *Halfsole* engine three times and would tune it again rather than replace it.

There used to be a fourth founding member. Marn does not say the name. The crew has learned not to ask.
sections.title: Named members the party knows about

sections.text:
- Carrier license active; Conclave ten-year audit due in **14 weeks**.
- Two ships: ***Wickrun*** (small kite-hauler, current primary) and ***Halfsole*** (older, in dock for engine work, expected back in two months).
- Co-op fund: enough for one major refit and one month of crew pay. Not enough for both.
- Owes a quarterly mortuary-cargo report to the Tempered Accord's death-registrar.
sections.title: Current standing

sections.text:
Each PC is on the books — a flying member of the second crew, a specialist, a fixer-in-training, a co-op apprentice, a recent hire, an owed favor working off a debt, whatever shape the player invents. The Run is your **home base**, not your employer in the strict sense. You have a vote at the co-op meeting. You also have a stake in the license surviving the audit.

What the Run needs is *kinds of leverage* — not a role chart. It needs people who can sit at a Conclave audit without flinching. People who can carry a sealed fragment past Coremark surveyors. People who can talk to a grieving family and not make it worse. People who read what nobody reads. People who can fly a kite-hauler in weather the others won't. Bring a character. The leverage will fit.
sections.title: How the party fits

sections.text: It's not a heroic outfit. The Run is a livelihood. It is also, occasionally, a quiet line of resistance against an institution that decides who gets to hear their own dead. Both things are true at once.

# Echo Ledger Conclave — Campaign Layer

The world-bible entry covers what the Conclave is: institutional custodian of pre-Glassfall material surfacing in the Echo Rivers, system-wide academic body, jealous of access. This file is what the Conclave is *doing right now in this campaign*.
sections.title: body

sections.text: The institutional witness who has been winning the access argument for ninety years and is starting to lose grip on the regional periphery.
sections.title: One-sentence identity

sections.text:
Pass the **Stillsink Catalogue Mandate** through the Conclave's own central council in the next four months. The Mandate would centralize all Sable Crescent fragment-cataloguing in Sithari and effectively shut down the regional Sable Office's autonomous releases — including private-commission carriage by independents like the Half-Bar Run.

The Mandate is being pushed from Sithari, opposed by the Sable Office, and quietly hated by the regional grievers' associations. The vote is mid-arc material.
sections.title: Goal — current

sections.text: System-wide, upper-middle. Not as large as Coremark or the Tempered Accord. More legitimacy than money.
sections.title: Tier

sections.text:
- Custodianship of every catalogued fragment surfacing in the Echo Rivers system-wide. Storage, access protocols, release schedules.
- Carrier license issuance and audit. Half-Bar Run's license is theirs.
- Stillwater buoy distribution rights along the Sable boundary.
- A respected academic publication record going back ninety years.
- The deep Conclave archive vault under Sithari, where the *original* recovered fragments live. Nobody outside the Conclave has been in there in twenty years.
sections.title: Hold

sections.text:
If nothing opposes the central council's push:

1. **Sable Office formally raises objection** — done; this is where the campaign starts. The objection is procedural and weak.
2. **Council circulates a draft.** Draft includes a "private-commission grandfather clause" that excludes existing carriers — the Half-Bar Run is *not* protected because their license was renewed two years ago and the clause cuts off at three years.
3. **Council vote scheduled.** Sable Office begins quietly preparing to leave the Conclave entirely. (This is real. They will.)
4. **Mandate passes.** Sable Office secedes or capitulates. Either way, the Half-Bar Run loses its private-commission carriage rights at the next renewal.

If the party engages: the clock can be slowed, redirected, or the Sable Office's secession can be steered toward something less catastrophic. If the party doesn't: the world keeps moving.
sections.title: Clock — Stillsink Catalogue Mandate (4 segments)

sections.text:
- **Coremark Reach Survey:** opposed in principle, cooperative in practice. Conclave gets first look at Coremark river-rock cores; Coremark gets a Conclave seal that lets them sell the processed feedstock at academic-grade prices.
- **Tempered Accord:** Accord-recognized; quietly resentful of Accord oversight attempts.
- **The Recursive Weave:** the Conclave officially does not acknowledge the Weave exists. They cancel members' licenses when caught dealing with it.
- **The Half-Bar Run:** known, watched, currently protected because no audit has caught the breaches. Audit is in 14 weeks.

# Coremark Reach Survey — Campaign Layer

The world-bible entry covers Coremark — system-wide industrial extractor, complicit in the Bloom origin, still legitimate, still profitable. This file is the **Reach Survey** subsidiary doing river-rock work in the Sable Crescent right now.
sections.title: body

sections.text: The Sable Crescent's largest single employer outside Sithari, harvesting crystal-saturated geology at industrial scale and treating the voices coming up with the rock as a regulatory cost.
sections.title: One-sentence identity

sections.text:
Open the **Springreed Cut** — a new extraction site upstream of Stillsink that would expand operations by an estimated 40%. The Cut requires a Tempered Accord environmental review and a Conclave fragment-recovery agreement. They're maneuvering both right now.

The Cut is the engine of the season. Whatever the party does, Coremark is pushing on this.
sections.title: Goal — current

sections.text: System-wide, large. Wealthy. Local political access. Real lawyers.
sections.title: Tier

sections.text:
- The **Reach Survey** processing facility at the eastern edge of the Sable Crescent, including the only large-scale crystal-water separation works on the surface.
- Permits to extract from twelve registered sites. Three of those sites are within audible range of named Echo River surfacings.
- A long-term feedstock contract with a major Sithari ringglass manufacturer. The manufacturer doesn't ask where the rock comes from.
- A "community engagement office" in Lowstep that holds quarterly public consultations, scheduled at 14:00 on weekdays in Sithari (six hours' travel each way from Lowstep).
- Real, expensive, under-the-table relationships with two members of the Conclave central council — both of whom are voting in favor of the Stillsink Catalogue Mandate.
sections.title: Hold

sections.text:
1. **Submit Accord environmental review.** Filed. Currently in 60-day comment period.
2. **Sign Conclave fragment-recovery agreement.** In negotiation. Coremark gets cataloguing rights over fragments their extraction surfaces; Conclave gets first publication rights and a 6% material fee.
3. **Break ground.** Earthmoving teams stage at Springreed. Local population at Lowstep is informed via a posted notice.
4. **First extraction.** River-rock cores begin processing. Stillsink's surfacing point is permanently degraded — voices that were audible there for forty years simply stop coming up. Nobody catalogues this.
5. **Operations stable.** Reach Survey adds a second shift. Five-year extraction projection met inside two years.

The party can intervene at multiple segments. They cannot stop Coremark *as an organization*. They can stop, slow, or redirect this specific Cut.
sections.title: Clock — Springreed Cut (5 segments)

sections.text:
- **Echo Ledger Conclave:** institutionally opposed, transactionally cooperative. The Mandate that hurts the Half-Bar Run is, ultimately, easier for Coremark to negotiate against than the Sable Office's autonomy.
- **Tempered Accord:** Accord audits Coremark every five years. Coremark pays the fines and continues.
- **The Recursive Weave:** Coremark's security has identified three Weave-affiliated dockhands at Lowstep and is letting them keep operating in exchange for early intelligence.
- **The Half-Bar Run:** non-priority. Coremark Reach Survey would prefer they not exist but does not consider them a strategic threat. That assessment can change.
sections.title: Relationships

sections.text: Coremark's "community engagement" is structurally inaccessible. Quarterly consultations at 14:00 on weekdays in Sithari, with two-week registration windows that close at 09:00 the morning of the meeting. Real, on-paper, legal. The information asymmetry is the point — they'd rather satisfy the form than the substance. Lowstep has not had a single resident attend a Coremark consultation in three years. The Tempered Accord knows. The Tempered Accord has filed eleven complaints. Each complaint resulted in a written response. None of the responses changed the schedule.
sections.title: Texture (web pull)

sections.text:
---
title: The Recursive Weave
type: faction
status: working
---

# The Recursive Weave

A distributed, leaderless network of grieving families, rogue archivists, ex-Conclave field workers, and a small number of fae — distinguished by the fact that nobody at the top knows everyone at the bottom. Operates the only reliable underground distribution channel for Echo River fragments outside Conclave control.
sections.title: body

sections.text: The pirate library that gives families their dead back, sometimes recklessly.
sections.title: One-sentence identity

sections.text: Place a working Echo River cataloguing-and-distribution node at **Stillsink** before the Coremark Cut destroys the surfacing point. They are six weeks out from operational. They need a carrier they can trust to ferry their starter fragment-archive to a hidden cache. They have approached Marn Vesh once. She did not say no.
sections.title: Goal — current

sections.text: Regional, small. Disproportionate political reach because every Sable Crescent grieving family has heard of them.
sections.title: Tier

sections.text:
- A loose web of caches in the Sable lowlands and one at Fermata Station.
- Trusted private buyers in three Sithari neighborhoods.
- A working catalogue, kept on rotating ringglass shards, of fragments the Conclave has refused or delayed.
- The trust of about 200 families across the Crescent.
- Three Coremark dockhands at Lowstep who pass them early extraction schedules.
sections.title: Hold

sections.text:
1. **Site identified.** Done. The node will sit in the lower channels of the abandoned Holdcells, where pre-Glassfall fragment-shards were stashed two generations ago and never retrieved.
2. **Carrier secured.** Currently negotiating. The Half-Bar Run is the candidate.
3. **Starter archive moved.** A single high-priority shipment, four crates, mortuary-shaped to deflect inspection.
4. **Antenna array bonded.** Tuner work on a buried river-channel. Specialist required.
5. **Node live.** Public *if you know who to ask*. Shifts the regional fragment economy permanently.

If Coremark breaks ground at Springreed before the node is live, the Stillsink surfacing degrades and the node becomes a museum of dead fragments.
sections.title: Clock — Stillsink Node Operational (5 segments)

sections.text:
- **Echo Ledger Conclave:** mutual non-acknowledgment. The Conclave will not name them. The Weave will not formally challenge them. Both sides know this is a fragile equilibrium.
- **Coremark Reach Survey:** opposed. The Weave's three Coremark contacts are double-edged — the contacts feed Weave intelligence, but Coremark security has identified them and is currently using them.
- **Tempered Accord:** the Accord *unofficially* prefers the Weave exists. Officially, the Accord prosecutes when caught doing so.
- **The Half-Bar Run:** approached, courted, partially trusted. Marn Vesh and a Weave handler called **Pell** have a long phone-tag relationship.
sections.title: Relationships

sections.text: The Weave is not virtuous. They have given a fragment to the wrong family — twice, that anyone knows. A 2131 incident at Fermata returned a child's voice to a grandfather who had never met the child and had no claim on her; he listened to the fragment for eleven straight days and then sealed himself in a stillwater room for six. The Weave does not talk about this. The Conclave does, when it serves them.
sections.title: Texture

sections.text:
---
title: Marn Vesh
type: npc
culture: hab-worlder
status: working
---

# Marn Vesh

Hab-Worlder, mid-forties. Founder and current head of the Half-Bar Run. Former Conclave field-archivist, fired in 2114 for an unsanctioned listen. The Run's eight-person crew think she "washed out." She did not. She let them think it.
sections.title: body

sections.text: She carries a chipped enamel mug to every meeting and sets it down deliberately. Soft set: the conversation is going to keep going. Hard set: the meeting is over and she's already decided.
sections.title: Tell

sections.text:
- **Near:** clear the Conclave audit (14 weeks). Pay off the *Wickrun* refit.
- **Mid:** decide whether to take the Recursive Weave's Stillsink-node carriage offer. She has been not-deciding for six weeks.
sections.title: Wants

sections.text: - That the Run becomes a cult of personality and dies with her bad lungs. She is already coughing on cold mornings; the crew has noticed.
sections.title: Fears

sections.text:
- Was an apprentice to **Auren Talindra** at the Sable Office in 2110-2114. They have not spoken since 2114. Auren still files Run audits.
- Owes a personal debt to **Tev Korrish** (Lowstep dockmaster) whose brother went to drift on a Half-Bar Run job in 2129.
- Has a long phone-tag relationship with **Pell**, the Weave handler. Marn calls; Pell does not always call back. Marn finds this restful.
sections.title: Bond / hook

sections.text: This week, if no PCs touch her: she takes a slow walk around *Wickrun*'s berth at Lowstep, drinks one cup of tea at Tev's stall, and reschedules the Pell call for the third time. Next week she writes a letter to Auren that she will not send.
sections.title: Next move

sections.text:
---
title: Auren Talindra
type: npc
culture: sithari
status: working
---

# Auren Talindra

Sitharian, late fifties. Senior field-archivist at the Conclave's Sable Office, Lowstep branch. Files all Half-Bar Run audits personally — has done since 2110, when Marn Vesh was her apprentice.
sections.title: body

sections.text: Wears a folded paper note in her left sleeve, never the right. The note is a reminder she rewrites every morning. Whatever it says, she touches it before she signs anything contested. PCs will see her touch her sleeve and not know why.
sections.title: Tell

sections.text:
- **Near:** to keep the Sable Office autonomous. To file a Run audit that closes clean. To finish the Mereth Korvanis catalogue project before she retires.
- **Mid:** to retire to a Sable Crescent fishing settlement with her partner. Within four years.
sections.title: Wants

sections.text: - That the Stillsink Catalogue Mandate passes and the Sable Office secedes from the Conclave. She would have to choose. Her partner expects her to choose Sable. Her professional life expects Sithari.
sections.title: Fears

sections.text:
- Marn Vesh's former mentor. They have not spoken since Marn's firing in 2114. Auren signed the dismissal. She still keeps Marn's apprenticeship-year fragment notebook in her office; nobody knows.
- Sits two chairs away from one of the two Conclave council members in Coremark's pocket — has noticed the woman's voting drift but has not yet acted on it.
sections.title: Bond / hook

sections.text: Files a routine Run audit-prep notice on schedule. Touches her sleeve. Files it.
sections.title: Next move

sections.text:
---
title: Pell
type: npc
culture: hab-worlder
status: working
---

# Pell

Hab-Worlder, no last name on any record (intentional). Recursive Weave handler for the Sable Crescent. Late thirties, looks fifty.
sections.title: body

sections.text: Smokes a hand-rolled wrap of dried saberreed that smells, faintly, like ringglass dust. Smokes it down to nothing in deliberate stages.
sections.title: Tell

sections.text:
- **Near:** lock in a Half-Bar Run carriage contract for the Stillsink-node starter archive. Six weeks to do it.
- **Mid:** put the node live before Coremark breaks ground at Springreed. Three months.
sections.title: Wants

sections.text: - The 2131 Fermata incident — a fragment given to the wrong grandfather — repeating. She was the handler who approved that release. She is the only Weave member who knows the full story. The Conclave has it as leverage.
sections.title: Fears

sections.text:
- Phone-tag with Marn Vesh.
- Knows two of the three Coremark dockhands the Weave is using as informants are also feeding Coremark security. She has not told the third dockhand. The third dockhand is **Tev Korrish's** niece.
sections.title: Bond / hook

sections.text: Writes a third letter to Marn this week. Does not send it. Drafts a contingency plan for finding a different carrier.
sections.title: Next move

sections.text:
---
title: Vendrik Solvaen
type: npc
culture: sithari
antagonist: true
status: working
---

# Vendrik Solvaen

Sitharian, mid-forties. Coremark Reach Survey site lead for the Sable Crescent. Reports directly to Coremark central in Sithari. Lives on-site at Reach Survey HQ; visits Sithari one weekend a month.

He is not a villain. He is a competent industrial manager who took the job because his daughter has a chronic resonance-damage condition that requires Sithari-grade hospital care, which Coremark's executive medical plan covers. He believes the Cut is the right call. He believes the Sable Crescent communities are being unreasonable. He believes both things sincerely.
sections.title: body

sections.text: Wears a small ringglass pendant around his neck, never visible — the chain disappears under his collar. The pendant is a tuned shard that hums when his daughter is awake (paired-resonance keepsake; Lyse, age nine, calibrated it as a school project two years ago). PCs who get close enough to see his throat will see the chain. PCs who get into a quiet room with him will hear the pendant.
sections.title: Tell

sections.text:
- **Near:** sign the Conclave fragment-recovery agreement. Break ground at Springreed. (Faction clock segments 2-3.)
- **Mid:** advance to Coremark central in Sithari within two years.
sections.title: Wants

sections.text: - His daughter's condition worsening. The job covers her care. He cannot afford to lose it.
sections.title: Fears

sections.text:
- Reports up to **Mer Korvanis** (Coremark central, in Sithari). Mer is one of the two council members feeding Coremark intel on the Stillsink Catalogue Mandate.
- Has personally hired Pell-affiliated dockhand **Joren Pell** (no relation to the Weave handler) and is using him to pass selective intelligence to the Weave. He thinks of this as "managing the unmanageable."
sections.title: Bond / hook

sections.text:
- **HP range:** 18 (he is not a fighter; he is an organizer with armed contractors).
- **Attack profile:** unarmed, untrained — but never alone. Two Reach Survey security personnel within hailing distance whenever he is on-site (each: HP 22, kite-cord net + signal-disrupter baton).
- **What makes him dangerous:** institutional reach, not personal force. He can have the Half-Bar Run's berth declared an "operational hazard zone" with three signatures and a phone call. He can have *Wickrun*'s license flagged for review. He can do these things faster than the party can react.
- **How he escapes if losing:** calls in security, then a phone call. He has never been in an actual physical confrontation. He will run.
sections.title: Stat-block

sections.text: This week: completes the Accord environmental review's required public consultation (in Sithari, 14:00 Tuesday). Reads the meeting minutes himself before filing them. Notes that nobody from Lowstep attended.
sections.title: Next move

sections.text:
Mirrors Coremark Reach Survey's faction clock; this is the *personal* version. He advances when he closes a procedural step. Tied to:

1. Accord review filed → 1/5
2. Conclave agreement signed → 2/5
3. Ground broken → 3/5
4. First extraction → 4/5
5. Operations stable → 5/5

When Vendrik's clock hits 5, he is promoted to Sithari, and the next Reach Survey lead is someone the party has never met.
sections.title: Vendrik's clock — Springreed Cut Realized (5 segments)

sections.text:
---
title: Yorek
type: npc
culture: hab-worlder
status: working
---

# Yorek

Hab-Worlder, sixty-eight. Mononym in practice; "Yorek Sands" on legal papers. Retired kite-cord weaver from a small Sable settlement called Sands End that no longer formally exists (absorbed into Lowstep's administrative district six years ago). A recurring Half-Bar Run client.
sections.title: body

sections.text: Wears one good wool coat that was his daughter's. The coat is two sizes too small for him. He never closes the buttons.
sections.title: Tell

sections.text:
- **Near:** the Run carries a fragment for him. He has paid for three so far. He wants a fourth. The Conclave has refused his fourth on procedural grounds (insufficient family-tree documentation; Sands End records were lost in 2129).
- **Mid:** to die having heard his daughter's last sentence. He has the first three sentences. He believes there's a fourth.
sections.title: Wants

sections.text: - That the fourth fragment doesn't exist. That his daughter never said anything more. That what he wants is not real.
sections.title: Fears

sections.text:
- A regular at Tev Korrish's tea stall. They sit together once a week and do not talk about Tev's brother or about Yorek's daughter.
- Has approached the Recursive Weave once. Pell turned him down — the Weave does not deal in fragments without strong family-tree evidence either, *anymore*, after the 2131 Fermata incident.
sections.title: Bond / hook

sections.text: This week: returns to the Sable Office in person (he does this every fourth week) and asks again for a procedural review. Auren Talindra signs his refusal personally. He thanks her. He leaves.
sections.title: Next move

sections.text:
---
title: Tev Korrish
type: npc
culture: hab-worlder
status: working
---

# Tev Korrish

Hab-Worlder, fifty-three. Lowstep's senior dockmaster. Runs the small tea stall at the foot of Berth 4 — *Wickrun*'s berth — which is technically a code violation that nobody has cited him for in fifteen years.
sections.title: body

sections.text: Closes one eye when he's tallying. The other eye is a clear blue and unsettling to be tallied by.
sections.title: Tell

sections.text:
- **Near:** keep the dock running. Keep the small berths from being rolled up into Coremark's expansion plans (he has been hearing rumors).
- **Mid:** marry off his niece to someone she actually likes. (His niece is **Joren Pell**, the dockhand, who he does not know is feeding Coremark security and the Weave simultaneously.)
sections.title: Wants

sections.text: - His brother, who went to drift in 2129 on a Half-Bar Run mortuary job, was not safe-recovered. Tev believes — has come to believe — that his brother is partly inside the *Wickrun* tuner array. He has never said this to Marn. He once asked Joro about the array's harmonic profile. Joro, kindly, gave a non-answer.
sections.title: Fears

sections.text:
- Marn Vesh owes him a debt. He has never collected it. He keeps it in his pocket like the chipped enamel mug.
- His niece Joren Pell is a dockhand and, unbeknownst to Tev, a triple agent (Coremark, Weave, the dock).
sections.title: Bond / hook

sections.text: This week: hears, from a Coremark site clerk who ate at his stall, that the Reach Survey expansion may include a "berth consolidation" review. Says nothing. Closes the stall an hour early Friday. Walks the dock.
sections.title: Next move

sections.text:
---
title: Drift-Eels
type: creature
status: working
---

# Drift-Eels

Native to Echo River surfacing pools and the marshy lowlands of the Sable Crescent. Soft-bodied, pale, sightless, mid-band ringglass parasites — they ingest free-floating signal-band crystal dust and excrete it in oriented ribbons, which is part of how the rivers' surfacings stay productive.
sections.title: body

sections.text: Wherever an Echo River pool sits open to air for more than an hour. Lowstep's edge. Stillsink. The lower channels of the Holdcells. Anywhere a tuned ringglass array runs cold near standing water.
sections.title: Where encountered

sections.text: Slow. Curious. Drawn to active resonance — a singing tuner-array, a fragment-shard that's been improperly sealed, a kite-hauler whose engine has been left humming overnight. They do not bite. They *attach* — soft-mouthed suction, harmless to skin, but if attached to a tuned crystal they slowly drift its calibration. A drift-eel on a fragment-shard is a six-hour problem. A drift-eel on a *Wickrun*-grade tuner array is a six-week problem.
sections.title: Behavior

sections.text: Pale, wet, faintly humming at 3.1-3.4 kHz — *just* in audible range as a low buzz under the smell of wet rock. They feel rubbery and warm. They smell, faintly, like ringglass dust and old water.
sections.title: Sense

sections.text:
- HP 4, no damaging attack.
- A crew that doesn't seal a fragment after listening invites drift-eel attachment in any wet location. Treat as a Calibration Drift consequence (ticks a clock; resolved by Tuner specialist, mid-band band, ~4 hours).
- An infestation in a tuner-array is a Major Equipment Consequence — three days minimum out of service, qualified Tuner needed.
- They are, technically, edible. Sable locals do not eat them.
sections.title: Mechanical hooks

sections.text: To attach. That's it. They are wildlife.
sections.title: What they want

sections.text:
---
title: The Antiphon
type: creature
status: working
---

# The Antiphon

Singular, named, recurring. Bloom-corrupted resonance entity that wears the voices of the dead. Uncategorized by the Conclave; informally documented by the Bloom Containment Office as Anomaly SC-11.

There may be others like it. The Antiphon is the only one that has shown a pattern.
sections.title: body

sections.text: Sable Crescent surface, primarily at degraded Echo River surfacings — places where Coremark extraction or natural geology change has *partially* destroyed a surfacing point. The Antiphon prefers ruined acoustics. It avoids healthy pools and avoids the deep Bloom Zone equally.
sections.title: Where encountered

sections.text:
Approaches alone. Speaks first. Says something the listener has heard before — sometimes a real fragment from a real river, sometimes a phrase the listener said yesterday and assumed unheard. Asks a question. Listens to the answer. Repeats it.

If engaged: extends. The voice multiplies. The listener experiences what the Conclave calls *deep drift* — uncertainty about which voice is theirs.

If left alone: walks away. Has been seen leaving a surfacing point with what looks like a small ringglass shard in its hand. Has not been seen using one.
sections.title: Behavior

sections.text: Looks like a person at a distance. Up close, the proportions are wrong by about ten percent — slightly taller, slightly thinner, hands slightly long. Speaks in *stitches* of borrowed voice — a syllable in one cadence, the next syllable in another. PCs who have heard a relevant fragment will sometimes recognize a voice in the stitching. Smells like wet rock and the inside of a sealed container.
sections.title: Sense

sections.text:
- HP 28. Resonance-band entity; physical attacks deal half damage unless the attacker is using a *tuned* weapon.
- Attack profile: a Voice attack at signal-band, range 6m, target makes a Composure save. On failure: target loses one piece of speech they remember saying — gone, not retrievable. The Antiphon now has it.
- Mid-band aversion: a sustained mid-band tone (instrument, properly tuned tuner-array) deals 2/round and the Antiphon retreats at 50% HP.
- Cannot be killed in the conventional sense. Reduced to 0 HP, it disperses and reforms in a different surfacing within 7-30 days. The campaign's question of *whether killing it is the right answer* is a real one.
sections.title: Mechanical hooks

sections.text:
A 4-segment soft clock. Each segment ticks when the Antiphon successfully takes a piece of borrowed speech from a named PC or NPC.

1. The Antiphon begins repeating PC speech in unrelated scenes (heard by an NPC; the PC does not know).
2. A Conclave field-archivist catalogues the repetition. Word gets back.
3. The Antiphon shows up at the Half-Bar Run berth speaking in Marn's voice.
4. The Antiphon has enough pattern to *predict* — to say a sentence the PC was about to say before they say it.

This clock isn't about defeating the Antiphon. It's about whether the campaign decides what the Antiphon *is*. The DM does not pre-decide.
sections.title: Clock — The Antiphon Speaks (for the campaign)

sections.text: I do not know. I will play to find out.
sections.title: What it wants

sections.text:
---
title: Wickrun
type: ship
status: working
---

# Wickrun

The Half-Bar Run's primary kite-hauler. Two crew bunks, four-berth common, a small mortuary bay sealed in lead-lined ringglass, a tuner array bonded into the hull frame.
sections.title: body

sections.text: A short-haul kite-hauler in the older Sable working class — squat, wide-bellied, faded blue paint with the *Wickrun* glyph hand-painted in white on the port side by Marn Vesh in 2117. Resonance-bonded ringglass array along the underside of the hull. Looks unremarkable in flight. Sounds wrong if you know what you're listening for.
sections.title: Description

sections.text: Built 2089 in a Lowstep yard, original name *Tarnsail*. Sold three times. Bought by Marn Vesh in 2117 from a Conclave estate sale (the estate being one of the founding Conclave archivists; the ship had been used as a deep-archive transport for fifteen years). Marn renamed it *Wickrun* and refit the tuner array with help from **Eshaia** (former Half-Bar Run member, fae, no longer with the co-op).
sections.title: Provenance

sections.text: Active. Berth 4 at Lowstep dock. Cord set at ~30% wear (durable public clock: *Wickrun: Cord Wear* at 1/5). Hull integrity good. Tuner array calibrated to a profile nobody alive can fully replicate — Eshaia's hand. Joro has tried twice. The array still rings cold to her tuning.
sections.title: Current status

sections.text:
- **Mid-band-tuned kite-hauler.** Engine cores tuned to 2.8 kHz with a half-bandwidth of 80 Hz. Quieter than industry standard. Slower in hard wind.
- **Mortuary cargo bay** sealed for resonance-bonded ash and Echo River fragments. Lead-lined. The seal is rated for Conclave-grade fragment carriage.
- **Tuner array** in the hull frame, primary purpose: cancel ambient signal-band noise so cargo doesn't drift in transit. Side effect: anyone with Tuner training who runs the array cold can occasionally hear *Eshaia's* calibration as a thin overtone. PCs with the right specialty will notice.
sections.title: What it does

sections.text: The array is unique. Coremark has, twice, asked about acquiring or licensing it. Marn has refused both times. The third time will be different. Also — the ship has *witnessed* every Half-Bar Run job since 2117. Voices have passed through that mortuary bay. Some of those voices are still, very faintly, in the lead.
sections.title: Hook

sections.text:
---
title: The Mereth Shard
type: artifact
status: working
---

# The Mereth Shard

A pre-Glassfall ringglass communication shard, palm-sized, with a tuned crack running diagonally across its face. Holds a single sealed fragment — a recording of an elven voice speaking the name *Mereth Korvanis* and one full sentence after it. The sentence is not in any modern language. The Conclave's central archive holds the master record; the field copy is a Sable Office responsibility.
sections.title: body

sections.text: Cold to the touch. Hums at 0.4 kHz when active — almost subsonic, felt in the chest more than heard. The crack is *deliberate* — old elven engineering used controlled fractures to lock specific frequency profiles. The shard cannot be re-tuned without destroying it.
sections.title: Description

sections.text: Surfaced in 2098 at Stillsink. Catalogued by an Auren Talindra-led field team in 2099. Auren personally maintained the field copy until 2114. Currently in the Sable Office's tertiary vault, accessible to Auren and one other archivist.
sections.title: Provenance

sections.text: Field copy in vault. Master in Sithari's deep Conclave archive. The shard has been requested four times by Conclave central since 2120; the Sable Office has stalled four times. The Stillsink Catalogue Mandate, if passed, requires the field copy be transferred to Sithari within 30 days of the vote.
sections.title: Current status

sections.text:
- Played in a properly tuned listening room, the shard speaks the name and the sentence. Eight seconds of audio.
- Mereth Korvanis was a Sitharian — a name the Continuity recognizes — but the *recording is elven*. An elf, two centuries ago, said a Sitharian name with the kind of intimacy that suggests they knew her. This is the load-bearing fact. The Conclave has not published it.
- Played in an *improperly* tuned room: drift-risk significantly elevated.
sections.title: What it does

sections.text: If the Sable Office secedes, the shard goes with it. If it stays in Sithari, it gets buried. If it ends up in private hands, the Recursive Weave can copy it and the Conclave will burn the world to recover it. The party will be the ones moving it, regardless of who wins the political fight.
sections.title: Hook

sections.text:
---
title: The Null Bell
type: artifact
status: working
---

# The Null Bell

A small hand-bell, brass, with a ringglass clapper. Tuned to *cancel* — when struck, produces a 5-second window of mid-band silence in a 4m radius. The Half-Bar Run's specialty equipment, used to seal a fragment-shard in transit if it begins to drift.
sections.title: body

sections.text: Looks ordinary. Brass shows wear from forty years of use. The clapper is grey-blue ringglass with a chip taken out of the bottom. Strikes with a sound like *un-ringing* — a sudden absence rather than a tone.
sections.title: Description

sections.text: Made in 2093 by a Sithari instrument-maker named **Velin Doraleth** for the Conclave's mobile carriage program. One of twelve. Six are still tracked; six are unaccounted for. The Half-Bar Run owns *number 9*, purchased from a retiring Conclave field-archivist in 2118 — the bell was the archivist's farewell gift to herself.
sections.title: Provenance

sections.text: Held in the *Wickrun*'s mortuary bay seal-locker. Used 4-6 times a year. Never replaced; cannot be replaced without commissioning a new one from a current instrument-maker, and Velin Doraleth's tuning notes were lost in 2129.
sections.title: Current status

sections.text:
- Striking the bell produces 5 seconds of mid-band cancellation in a 4m radius. During those five seconds, a drifting fragment-shard re-stabilizes; an Echo River pool stops humming; an active drift-eel detaches. The bell does not work on the Antiphon (untested but assumed; nobody has been close enough to try).
- Cost: each strike scratches the clapper microscopically. After ~200 strikes, the clapper fractures. Number 9 has, by Marn's records, been struck 168 times.
sections.title: What it does

sections.text: Coremark would like to know how many strikes are left on Bell 9. The Conclave would like to inventory all 12. The Recursive Weave would *significantly* like to have one of the unaccounted-for six.
sections.title: Hook

sections.text:
---
title: Lowstep
type: locale
status: working
---

# Lowstep

A working dock-and-warehouse town on the western edge of the Sable Crescent. Two thousand permanent residents. The Half-Bar Run's home berth.
sections.title: body

sections.text: Wet stone underfoot most mornings. The smell of crystal sediment from the lower river-channels mixes with kite-cord hemp and frying saberreed. The dock pilings still carry the pre-Glassfall foundations — every fourth pylon hums faintly when wind comes off the water.
sections.title: What it feels like

sections.text:
- **Berth 4** is *Wickrun*'s. Tev Korrish's tea stall sits at the foot of the berth, technically a code violation. The kettle has been on a continuous low burn for fifteen years.
- The **Conclave Sable Office** is up the slope from the docks, in a converted ring-era administrative building. Three floors. The third floor is restricted. Auren Talindra's office is on the second.
- The **Coremark Reach Survey "community engagement office"** is on the main commercial street, posted hours, never staffed when those hours match the dock schedule. Locals do not enter.
sections.title: What's worth writing

sections.text:
- Dockhands, including **Joren Pell** (Tev's niece, triple agent: Coremark security, Recursive Weave, the dock).
- Half-Bar Run crew. Marn lives in a small flat above the berth office.
- Yorek, every fourth Tuesday at the Sable Office. Tev's tea stall after.
- Coremark Reach Survey contractors transiting through; not residents but visible.
sections.title: Who's usually there

sections.text: - Every fourth pier-pylon hums in resonance with a distant Echo River channel that surfaces 11km inland. Locals have used the hum as a weather-tell for two generations.
sections.title: What does something

sections.text:
---
title: Stillsink
type: locale
status: working
---

# Stillsink

The largest active Echo River surfacing point in the Sable Crescent. A cold spring that opens onto a rock pool ringed by exposed crystal-bearing stone, with a small boardwalk built by the Conclave in 2110 to manage listener access.
sections.title: body

sections.text: The water is cold enough to hurt your hand inside ten seconds. The pool is calm; the air around it carries voices in layers — most unintelligible, some heartbreakingly clear. Conclave protocol is *no listener stays more than thirty minutes*. Most experienced visitors stay twenty.
sections.title: What it feels like

sections.text:
- The **boardwalk** has a sign-in book. Auren Talindra signed it 412 times between 2110 and 2114.
- The pool's **eastern lip** is fractured ringglass that someone, in 2089, tried to seal with a tuned brass plate. The plate is still there. The seal partially failed in 2127. Voices from before the seal occasionally surface clearer than voices from after.
- The boardwalk railings have small notches carved into them. Each notch is a listener's count of fragments they've personally heard. Auren's section runs 248 notches.
sections.title: What's worth writing

sections.text:
- One Conclave field monitor on rotation.
- Visiting researchers, by appointment.
- Grieving families with active permits (rare; bureaucratic).
- A small population of drift-eels in the lower channels.
sections.title: Who's usually there

sections.text:
- The Stillsink pool is the load-bearing surfacing point for the regional fragment economy. Coremark's Springreed Cut, four segments away on the faction clock, will permanently degrade it.
- Marn Vesh listened to a fragment here in 2114 that she was not authorized to listen to. The fragment has not surfaced since. Auren's notebook (the one she keeps in her office) records the fragment's frequency profile but not its content.
sections.title: What does something

sections.text:
---
title: The Holdcells
type: locale
status: working
---

# The Holdcells

A defunct enforcement house in the marshy lowlands south of Lowstep. Pre-Glassfall construction; ring-era stone, original cell doors, the kind of building that was built to outlast its purpose.
sections.title: body

sections.text: Damp. Cold even in summer. The cells are small — two by three meters, four-meter ceilings — and the plumbing-channels in the walls still carry, faintly, the *original* signal-band noise from when the building was active. Standing in a cell is to stand in a small Echo surfacing.
sections.title: What it feels like

sections.text:
- **Eight cells** along a central corridor. Cells 3 and 5 have working doors; the rest are jammed open or sealed.
- The **lower channels** beneath the cells are partially flooded. Drift-eels are present in numbers. Crystal-saturated water has had two generations to soak the foundations.
- In **2089**, someone — name not in any official record — stashed a wooden crate of pre-Glassfall fragment-shards in Cell 4. The crate is still there. Nobody on either side of any current dispute knows the inventory. The Recursive Weave knows the *crate exists*. The Conclave does not.
- The Recursive Weave's planned Stillsink-node site is in the lower channels, accessed via Cell 7's collapsed floor.
sections.title: What's worth writing

sections.text:
- Nobody. The building is officially condemned.
- Occasional urban explorers.
- The **Antiphon** has been seen here once, by a Lowstep teenager in 2133 who told nobody. The Antiphon was sitting in Cell 4 with its hand on the wooden crate.
sections.title: Who's usually there

sections.text:
- The lower channels are a real, untapped Echo River surfacing — small, but *unmonitored*. The Weave is six weeks from making it operational.
- The wooden crate's contents include a fragment-shard that nobody alive has catalogued. What it holds is a play-to-find-out blank.

# Conclave Sable Office

The Echo Ledger Conclave's regional Sable Crescent branch, located in Lowstep's converted ring-era administrative building, three floors up the slope from the docks.
sections.title: body

sections.text: Old paper, fresh ink, slow. Three floors of cataloguing rooms, a sign-in desk, a tertiary vault on the second floor, a third floor nobody discusses. The walls are ring-era stone, three meters thick, naturally signal-band-quiet — fragments stored here drift more slowly than fragments anywhere else on the Crescent.
sections.title: What it feels like

sections.text:
- **Auren Talindra's office** on the second floor. Door open most hours. Her notebook collection lines the back wall, including the apprenticeship-year notebook of one **Marn Vesh**, kept on the third shelf from the top.
- The **tertiary vault** holds the field copies of regional fragment-shards, including the **Mereth Shard**. Two-key access; Auren and one other archivist.
- The **third floor** holds the Sable Office's *political* archive — internal correspondence with Sithari, dissenting opinions, planning material for the secession nobody is officially planning. Access by request, granted reluctantly.
sections.title: What's worth writing

sections.text:
- Auren Talindra (most days).
- Two junior field-archivists. One of them is a Coremark sympathizer; she does not know that everyone else has noticed.
- Yorek, every fourth Tuesday.
- A semi-regular flow of Half-Bar Run paperwork — sealing certificates, mortuary registrations, license-audit precursors.
sections.title: Who's usually there

sections.text:
- The signal-band-quiet stone walls. A fragment played in this building does not drift. A meeting held in this building cannot be eavesdropped on with conventional equipment.
- The Mereth Shard is here. Its master is in Sithari.

---
title: Secrets and Clues
status: working
---

# Secrets and Clues

Ten short facts the players don't know yet. Unattached to specific scenes. I'll deploy whichever fits whichever moment. Three Clue Rule: for the truly required ones, three independent surfaces.

---

1. **Marn Vesh was fired, not "washed out."** She listened to a fragment in 2114 that she was not authorized to listen to. The Conclave knows. They have not used it. The audit in 14 weeks is not — *yet* — a pretext, but it could become one. **Three clues required if this becomes load-bearing.** Sources: Auren Talindra's notebook on her shelf; Stillsink sign-in book entry for 2114-03-19; a former Sable Office janitor at Tev's tea stall.

2. **Eshaia, the missing fae founding member of the Run, calibrated *Wickrun*'s tuner array in a way nobody can replicate.** The array still rings to her hand. She is intermittently present somewhere. Whether she *left* the Run or *became diffuse and never came back* is undetermined. (Play-to-find-out.)

3. **Sela Pruth is skimming co-op funds.** She uses the money to pay the Recursive Weave for fragment-access for her own family. Marn suspects. The audit will find this if anyone goes looking. Three clues if needed: the off-books ledger in Sela's drawer; Pell mentioning a "regular customer" by accident; a Lowstep dockhand who saw Sela hand off marks at Berth 11.

4. **The Mereth Shard's recording is an elf speaking a Sitharian name with intimate knowledge.** The Conclave has not published this. If the master in Sithari is destroyed and the field copy intact, the Sable Office can dictate publication terms. (DM-knows.)

5. **The Recursive Weave's 2131 Fermata incident — the wrong grandfather — was not as straightforward as either side claims.** The Conclave has the full story; they got it from a Tempered Accord investigator. The grandfather was not the *wrong* grandfather. The fragment was deliberately mis-routed by a Conclave informant inside the Weave to discredit the network. Pell does not know this. (DM-knows; reveal carefully.)

6. **The Coremark wooden crate in the Holdcells.** Inventoried by no one alive. Contents include at least one shard with an elven voice on it that does *not* match any known fragment. (Play-to-find-out what it actually says.)

7. **Tev Korrish believes his brother is partly inside *Wickrun*'s tuner array.** Whether this is true is genuinely undecided — there *is* an unidentified harmonic in the array, and the brother *did* die on a Run job, and Eshaia's calibration *does* hold residue. Three different things. They might be the same thing. (Play-to-find-out.)

8. **Joren Pell, Tev's niece, is feeding intelligence to all three sides.** She thinks she is in control. Coremark security has identified her as a Weave contact. The Weave does not know she is feeding Coremark. The dock thinks she is just a dockhand.

9. **The Stillsink Catalogue Mandate is being pushed by two Conclave central council members in Coremark's pocket.** Auren Talindra has noticed the voting drift but has not acted. If she gets evidence and the Half-Bar Run helps her surface it, the Mandate vote can be turned. Three clues if needed: voting records visible in published council minutes; a leaked correspondence accessible via the Recursive Weave; Auren's own private notes once she trusts the party.

10. **The Antiphon has been seen at the Holdcells, in Cell 4, with its hand on the Coremark wooden crate.** The teenager who saw it told nobody. The teenager is, in 2135, working at Tev's tea stall on weekends. (Play-to-find-out: what does the Antiphon want with what's in the crate?)

frontmatter.title: Listening as the unit of right and wrong

sections.text:
---
title: Listening as the unit of right and wrong
status: working
---

# Listening as the unit of right and wrong

This campaign is *about* who gets to listen. That makes listening — as an act, as a stance, as a craft — the moral atom of every scene.

The world bible's *Who Remembers* theme says it well: the Echo Rivers carry voices nobody chose to preserve. They are the world's uncontrollable archive. The institutions try to control the uncontrollable; they always partly succeed and always partly fail. The campaign is set in that partial-success-partial-failure gap.

What this means at the table:

- **Adjudicate listening as a *choice*.** When a player says "I listen to the fragment," that is a beat. Treat it like a roll-worthy decision. Ask what they're listening *for*.
- **Honor refusals.** A player who decides *not* to listen is making the same kind of choice. Don't treat it as low-engagement. Sometimes the right answer is silence.
- **Witness has cost.** Drift is the mechanical version. Politically, every fragment a PC hears makes them a *witness* — meaning testifiable, meaning leverage, meaning at risk.
- **The Conclave's gatekeeping is not the bad guy.** It is sometimes the bad guy. It is also, sometimes, the only thing keeping the rivers from being strip-mined for sentiment. Don't write it as villain. Write it as institution — slow, prone to drift toward its own preservation, occasionally still good.
- **Coremark's argument is real.** Cheap ringglass keeps the system running. The Bloom Containment Office *uses* Coremark river-rock for its containment buoys. The Run's *Wickrun* tuner array is calibrated against ringglass that came, originally, from a Coremark feedstock yard. Don't let the table forget.

The campaign is not asking *should we listen?* It is asking *which listenings are righteous and which are theft, and who gets to decide?*

I will not decide. The play decides.
sections.title: body

entity.title: Listening as the unit of right and wrong

sections.text:
---
title: Hopecore with cost
status: working
---

# Hopecore with cost

This is the world bible's stance and I want to write it back into the campaign explicitly.

The world is broken in named, specific ways. The Bloom happened. The elves are gone. The Redaction cleaned what it could reach. The Continuity has been winning every election since the Glassfall and counts that as virtue. Coremark profits from a containment regime they helped cause. Most days, none of that is the *active* problem. Most days are the dock, the kettle, the kite-cord.

The campaign should hold both. The pier-pylons hum at Lowstep and the kettle is on at Tev's stall and a child is being named in the next neighborhood over and the Stillsink Catalogue Mandate is going to pass unless someone does something. The hopeful and the dread are not in tension; they are the same fabric.

What this means at the table:

- **Don't punish small joys.** A scene at Tev's stall about the tea being good is a real scene. Let it land.
- **Don't reward heroism over presence.** A character who *witnesses* a thing — really listens to a grieving father, really sees a Conclave clerk's small daily act of integrity — is doing the campaign's actual work.
- **Avoid grimdark drift.** When in doubt, the world is still feeding people, the buses still come, kids still get named. Things are not fine. Things are still going.
- **Bad endings are real.** Yorek may not get his fragment. Pell's node may not go live. The Mandate may pass. Mara should not arrange wins. She should arrange *consequences*.

This is hopecore, not happy-ending-core. The world is worth saving and is also still worth living in if it isn't saved.

# Cord and Mandate — opening arc plan

The opening arc. Six to ten scenes. The pressure is the audit and the Mandate, both arriving on the same six-week clock. The party will be asked, by all three factions, to do incompatible things.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
**Will the Half-Bar Run survive the audit, and what will it have become to do so?**

I do not know the answer. The two parts of the question are linked — the survival path and the integrity path are not the same path, and "survive" can mean *license intact and crew bought*, or *license intact and crew the same people they were*, or *license lost and the Run becomes something it wasn't*.
sections.title: 1. The stakes question

sections.text:
### Threat A — Vendrik Solvaen / Coremark Reach Survey

Site lead pushing the Springreed Cut. Has personal incentive (his daughter's medical care) and institutional reach (he can grief the Run's berth via the Tempered Accord faster than the Run can react).

- **Impulse:** *To close out the procedural sequence, sign by sign, until the Cut is operational.*
- **Between scenes:** advances the Coremark Reach Survey faction clock by one segment per scene that the party doesn't directly oppose. Files paperwork. Gives interviews. Does not personally engage.

### Threat B — The Stillsink Catalogue Mandate (institutional pressure)

Push from Conclave central in Sithari. Two council members in Coremark's pocket. The vote is mid-arc material.

- **Impulse:** *To centralize. To absorb the Sable Office. To withdraw private-commission carriage rights at next renewal.*
- **Between scenes:** the council circulates drafts, holds quiet hearings, schedules the vote. Auren Talindra writes letters and does not send them. The Mandate moves whether or not the party engages.

### Threat C — The audit pretext

The Conclave has Marn's 2114 firing on file. The audit is, technically, routine. It will become a pretext if the Run becomes politically inconvenient.

- **Impulse:** *To remain dormant unless triggered. Then to surface.*
- **Between scenes:** Auren preps the audit folder. The Conclave central liaison arrives at Lowstep mid-arc.

### Threat D — Pell's offer / the Stillsink-node opportunity

The Recursive Weave wants the Run to ferry the starter archive to the Holdcells. Six-week timeline before Coremark breaks ground at Springreed.

- **Impulse:** *To go live before the surfacing degrades. To give grieving families a route the Conclave has refused them.*
- **Between scenes:** Pell looks for alternate carriers if the Run delays past three weeks. She finds one in week four. The carrier she finds is bad.

The four threats share scarcity: **time** (six weeks), **trust** (everyone is asking the Run to keep secrets from someone), and **license** (Coremark can grief the berth, Conclave can pull the license, Weave can cost the Run both).
sections.title: 2. The threats

sections.text:
### Public clocks (already set, surfacing in this arc)

- **Wickrun: Cord Wear** — 5 segments, currently 1/5. Long Shear runs and weather-on-the-line runs tick.
- **Conclave 10-year audit** — 7 segments, currently 0/7. Audit-relevant scenes that go badly tick.

### Arc-specific clocks (set via `glass clock --scope arc`)

- **Springreed Cut** — 5 segments, currently 1/5 (Accord review filed). Mirrors faction clock; arc-anchored.
- **Stillsink Catalogue Mandate** — 4 segments, currently 1/4 (Sable Office objection raised).
- **Stillsink Node Operational** — 5 segments, currently 1/5 (site identified). Hidden public — known to the Weave; party knows when Pell tells them.

The arc resolves no later than when *Springreed Cut* hits 4/5 (first extraction begins) or *Conclave audit* hits 6/6 (license review under cause), whichever first.
sections.title: 3. Clocks

sections.text:
(Brainstorm only. Not picking.)

- **Audit cleared, Mandate passes, Cut breaks ground.** The Run survives but loses private-commission rights at next renewal. The Stillsink-node never goes live. Yorek does not get his fragment. The campaign continues, slower, sadder. (Quiet failure.)
- **Audit cleared, Mandate fails (party surfaces the Coremark voting drift).** Cut still breaks ground. Sable Office stays autonomous. Auren Talindra retires early; her replacement is worse. (Mixed.)
- **Audit clears, Mandate passes, Stillsink-node goes live.** Run is now operationally aligned with the Weave. Audit-clear was on the strict letter; future audits will be hostile. The party is now part of an underground. (A win that becomes a different campaign.)
- **Audit fails (a breach surfaces).** License revoked. Run dissolves; some crew go to the Weave, some go elsewhere. The campaign continues without the Run as institution — the Run becomes a memory of a thing the campaign was about. (Reshape.)
- **The Mereth Shard becomes the lever.** The party uses it (delivered, copied, suppressed, leaked) to settle the Mandate one way or the other. The arc becomes about the shard. (Likely if the party engages with Auren personally.)
- **Coremark's voting-drift evidence surfaces, the council members are removed, Vendrik is recalled to Sithari.** Reach Survey continues under a less-known lead. The Cut delays one season. The party has made an enemy of Coremark central. (A win that opens a future arc.)
sections.title: 4. Possible end-states

sections.text:
**Lowstep, Berth 4, mid-morning. *Wickrun* just back from a Stillsink fragment-run. Conclave audit-prep notice arrives at Marn's office (from Auren personally, hand-delivered with a coffee for Marn). At the same moment, Pell appears at the head of the dock, watching from a polite distance.**

Yorek is at Tev's stall.

The party arrives at the berth to unload. Marn reads the notice. Tev sees Pell. Marn sees Tev see Pell. Yorek waves at one of the PCs.

What do you do?

(Mug-on-the-table moment for Mara: scene ends one beat after the last commitment is made. Don't drag it.)
sections.title: 5. The strong start

sections.text:
### Node A — Berth 4 / *Wickrun*'s berth at Lowstep

The party's home base. Information available: dock gossip (Coremark consolidation rumor — Tev has not told Marn but might tell a PC); the audit notice on Marn's desk; *Wickrun*'s tuner-array harmonic anomaly (PCs with Tuner training will hear it).

- **Outbound clues:** to Conclave Sable Office (audit notice → Auren); to Stillsink (Yorek's request → fragment route); to the Holdcells (Pell mentions the Weave's site once if approached).

### Node B — Conclave Sable Office, Lowstep

Auren's office, the tertiary vault, the third floor. Information: the Mandate's draft text (Auren has it); Marn's apprenticeship notebook on the shelf (touchable, but only if Auren turns her back); the Mereth Shard's transfer order language (in a drawer, requires social engineering).

- **Outbound clues:** to Berth 4 (a copy of the audit prep folder); to Sithari (council voting records — the drift is in the public minutes if you read carefully); to the Holdcells (Auren has *not* told the Conclave about the unmonitored surfacing in the lower channels but knows it exists).

### Node C — Stillsink

The surfacing. Information: a fragment Yorek wants is in the catalogue but procedurally refused; the boardwalk sign-in book contains entries that contradict each other; the eastern-lip seal is failing slowly. Drift-eels in the lower channels.

- **Outbound clues:** to Conclave Sable Office (the catalogue index); to Stillsink (a junior Conclave monitor who is sympathetic to Yorek and shouldn't be); to the Holdcells (the surfacing geologically connects to the Holdcells lower channels).

### Node D — The Holdcells

Defunct enforcement house. Information: the wooden crate in Cell 4; the lower channels' unmonitored surfacing; Antiphon sighting history. Cold. Wet. No casual visitors.

- **Outbound clues:** to the Mereth Shard (one of the crate's contents references a Sitharian name); to Stillsink (the channels connect); to Pell (the Weave's intended node site).

### Node E — Coremark Reach Survey HQ, eastern Crescent

Vendrik's office. Information: Vendrik's pendant; the Cut's Springreed survey maps; the Conclave-pocket council member correspondence (in Vendrik's locked desk; Three-Clue-Rule item — also surfaceable from the Conclave Office and from the Recursive Weave's contacts).

- **Outbound clues:** to Conclave Sable Office (correspondence); to the Holdcells (Coremark security knows about the unmonitored surfacing too); to a future arc (Sithari council politics).

**Three-Clue check:** the council voting drift evidence — required for the Mandate-fails end-state — has three independent sources: the published council minutes (Conclave Sable Office), the leaked correspondence (Recursive Weave), Vendrik's locked desk (Coremark HQ). Three nodes. Three independent paths. Holds.
sections.title: 6. Nodes (5)

sections.text:
- **Antagonists:** Vendrik Solvaen.
- **NPCs:** Marn Vesh, Auren Talindra, Pell, Yorek, Tev Korrish.
- **Creatures:** Drift-eels (Holdcells, Stillsink). The Antiphon (possible Holdcells encounter; clock is campaign-level, not arc-level).
- **Named things:** *Wickrun*, the Mereth Shard, the Null Bell.
- **Locations:** Lowstep, Conclave Sable Office, Stillsink, the Holdcells. Coremark Reach Survey HQ stub (draft if play touches it).
- **Factions:** Echo Ledger Conclave, Coremark Reach Survey, Recursive Weave.
sections.title: 7. What from the curated lists is in play

sections.text:
(In addition to campaign-level `dm/notes/secrets.md`.)

- The audit notice Auren hand-delivered includes a small scrap of paper Marn will find slipped between page 4 and 5. The note is in Auren's hand. It says: *I have the notebook still. — A.* (Auren has not made up her mind whether to send this. Once delivered, can't be unsent.)
- Vendrik's daughter Lyse has, twice in the last year, left voicemails on her father's pendant from the school resonance lab. Both messages are stored, locally, in the pendant's small buffer. He has not deleted them. The party will not know about this unless they get *very close* to him.
- The wooden crate in the Holdcells contains, among other shards, *one* with an elven voice speaking what sounds like "Mereth" — a pre-Mereth-Shard fragment that the Conclave never catalogued. (DM-knows. If recovered, dramatically up-shifts the Mandate political calculus.)
- Joren Pell (Tev's niece) has, this week, recorded a conversation between Vendrik and a Sithari council member that names both pocket members on the Mandate vote. She is sitting on it. She has not decided who to give it to.
- A Conclave field monitor at Stillsink, **Sevaril**, has been quietly logging her own catalogue of fragments she thinks the Conclave is wrongly sealing. She has 22 entries. She does not know what to do with them.
sections.title: 8. Arc-specific secrets

sections.text:
The arc resolves when **the audit cycle closes (one way or another)** AND **the Springreed Cut is either operational, delayed by at least one season, or dead**.

Whichever comes second is the arc's ending beat.

I will abandon the arc if the party drifts away — declines the audit prep, refuses Pell's offer, doesn't engage Coremark — and three months pass off-screen. In that case, all three clocks resolve to their default end-states (audit clears narrowly; Mandate passes; Cut breaks ground), the Run loses private-commission rights at next renewal, and the campaign opens with a different shape on the table. That is also a real ending.

# test-5 — Campaign Summary

**Question:** Who has the right to listen?
**Scarcity:** witnesses
**Setting:** Sable Crescent, surface of Kaleidos. 130 years after the Glassfall.

The party belongs to **The Half-Bar Run**, a small carrier co-op out of Lowstep that ferries Echo River fragments and other resonance-sensitive cargo. They are halfway through a Conclave ten-year audit cycle, working under economic pressure (cord wear on *Wickrun*, *Halfsole* in dock), watching three institutional pressures converge: a Coremark expansion at Springreed, a Conclave Catalogue Mandate that would centralize cataloguing in Sithari, and a quiet offer from the Recursive Weave to ferry a starter archive to a new underground node.

The opening arc — **Cord and Mandate** — is the six-week window in which all three pressures resolve, one way or another.
